worth_it_explanation: Open Bidding partners each take a share of the auction, and Google's revenue share applies on top, so a partner that rarely wins or wins below the fee returns little while still adding auction overhead. The audit reads directly from Ad Manager reporting by yield partner, so it is quick to run and it shows which partners earn their place. Removing or repathing a partner that does not contribute is a configuration change in the account, not a rebuild. The result is a cleaner auction and a clearer picture of where programmatic revenue comes from.
intro: Open Bidding lets a publisher invite third-party exchanges to compete for inventory inside Google Ad Manager's unified auction, server-to-server, using the publisher's existing tags. It is simpler to operate than header bidding and adds less latency, but Google charges a revenue share on Open Bidding wins, so each partner has to return more than it costs to carry. These checks confirm that every active Open Bidding partner contributes positive net revenue, and that a partner running through both Open Bidding and header bidding is a deliberate choice rather than an unreviewed overlap.

audit_checks.title: Open Bidding partners are inactive or net-negative after fees
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A partner that rarely bids or wins, or whose wins do not cover Google's revenue share plus the partner's own take, returns little while still adding a callout to every auction. The partner reads as active in the yield group, so the shortfall does not announce itself, and the auction carries overhead without the revenue to justify it.

educate.base:
Open Bidding lets third-party exchanges compete for a publisher's inventory inside Google Ad Manager's unified auction. The exchanges sit in a yield group, Ad Manager calls each one for a bid when an ad request comes in, and the highest net bid wins. The bid is net because it already accounts for Ad Manager's revenue share, which Google charges on Open Bidding wins, so a partner's gross bid has to clear that share plus the partner's own margin before it returns anything to the publisher.

Adding a partner is close to free to set up, so yield groups tend to accumulate exchanges that were switched on to test demand and then never reviewed. A partner that rarely bids, that bids but rarely wins, or that wins at prices the revenue share mostly absorbs contributes little net revenue. Each partner Ad Manager has to call still adds a callout to the auction, so a partner that does not pull its weight is overhead without the income to justify it.

Ad Manager reporting separates this out by yield partner. The 'Yield Partner' and 'Demand Channel' dimensions show each partner's callouts, bids, auctions won, and revenue, so a partner can be judged on what it actually returns rather than on whether it is switched on. A partner with a high callout count and near-zero won revenue is a candidate to pause, and a partner whose revenue barely exceeds the fee it pays is worth a closer look at whether it adds demand the other partners were not already supplying.

A few signs point to a partner that is not earning its place:
  • The partner shows callouts and bids in reporting but wins almost no auctions
  • The partner wins, but its net revenue is a token amount next to the other partners
  • The partner was enabled to test and never appears in any revenue review since
You may want to compare each partner's net revenue against its share of the auction calls before deciding it earns its slot. A partner that reads as active is not the same as a partner that pays for the overhead it adds.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site, demand can vary by section and audience, so a partner that returns little overall may still win on a specific high-value section. You may want to read the yield partner report broken out by ad unit or placement before pausing a partner, rather than judging it on a single network-wide total.

investigate.intro: Pull Ad Manager reporting by yield partner and judge each Open Bidding partner on its net revenue against the auction calls it generates, rather than on whether it is switched on.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Delivery', then 'Yield groups', and list the Open Bidding partners currently enabled across your yield groups.

Open 'Reporting', then 'Reports', and build a report using the 'Yield Partner' and 'Demand Channel' dimensions with the callouts, bids, auctions won, and revenue metrics over a representative date range.

For each partner, note its callout count, its win count, and its net revenue. Flag any partner with many callouts and near-zero won revenue, and any partner whose revenue is a token amount next to the others.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per enabled Open Bidding partner, its callouts, its auctions won, and its net revenue over the date range reviewed. A partner that wins auctions and returns net revenue worth the callouts it adds passes. A partner that shows callouts but wins almost nothing, or whose net revenue is a token amount after the revenue share, is a fail recorded with the partner name and its revenue figure.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each Open Bidding partner's callouts, auctions won, and net revenue above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more Open Bidding partners on {{site_name}} are inactive or net-negative after fees, meaning they add a callout to every auction while returning little or no revenue once Google's revenue share is taken into account. Each partner Ad Manager has to call adds overhead to the auction, so a partner that wins almost nothing or wins below the fee it pays is cost without the income to justify it. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the yield partner report for each enabled partner, then pausing the partners that show callouts but near-zero won revenue and keeping the ones that return net revenue worth their slot. After the change, recheck the report to confirm the remaining partners each contribute positive net revenue.

audit_checks.title: Open Bidding and header bidding run the same partner without a comparison
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Running one exchange both server-side through Open Bidding and client-side through header bidding duplicates the auction work for that partner and can split its demand across two paths. Without a comparison of win rate, latency, and fees per path, the publisher cannot tell which route serves that partner better, so the duplication persists by default rather than by choice.

educate.base:
A publisher can route the same exchange through two paths into the Google Ad Manager auction. One path is Open Bidding, where Ad Manager calls the exchange server-to-server inside the unified auction. The other is header bidding, where the exchange runs client-side in the browser before the ad request reaches the ad server. Many large exchanges offer both, and a publisher who set up header bidding first and later enabled Open Bidding can end up with the same partner on both paths.

Running a partner on both paths duplicates the auction work for that partner and can split its demand. The same buyers may bid through the partner in the client-side auction and again through Ad Manager's server-side auction, which adds a redundant call and can mean the partner competes against itself rather than concentrating its demand in the path where it performs best. The two paths also differ in ways that matter. Header bidding runs in the browser and adds client-side latency, while Open Bidding runs server-side and carries Google's revenue share. The path that wins more, loads faster, or costs less is a question the reporting can answer per partner.

The point is not that one path is always better. It is that a partner on both paths should be a deliberate choice. A publisher may keep a partner on header bidding for the demand that path reaches and on Open Bidding for the simpler operation, but only after comparing how the partner performs on each. Left unreviewed, the overlap is a default that nobody decided on.

A few signs point to an overlap worth reviewing:
  • The same exchange appears in a yield group as an Open Bidding partner and in the header bidding wrapper configuration
  • The partner's win rate or revenue differs sharply between the two paths in reporting
  • The header bidding setup was built first and Open Bidding was added later without retiring any overlapping partners
You may want to compare each overlapping partner's win rate, latency, and net revenue per path before deciding to keep both. The goal is one deliberate path per partner, or two paths kept on purpose, rather than two paths kept by inertia.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a content site where page speed affects reading and ad viewability, the client-side latency that header bidding adds is worth weighing against the server-side path for an overlapping partner. You may want to compare each partner's contribution on each path against the load cost it adds, so the slower path is kept only where the demand justifies it.

investigate.intro: Identify the exchanges that appear in both Open Bidding and the header bidding setup, then compare each one's win rate and latency across the two paths.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Delivery', then 'Yield groups', and list the exchanges enabled as Open Bidding partners.
Review your header bidding wrapper configuration and list the exchanges running client-side. Mark any exchange that appears on both lists.

For each overlapping exchange, build an Ad Manager report with the 'Yield Partner' and 'Demand Channel' dimensions and compare its bids, auctions won, and revenue on the Open Bidding path against its header bidding line items.

google_publisher_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Open a page that loads ads and launch the Google Publisher Console from the page.
Inspect the auction and bidder timing to see how long the client-side header bidding partners take to respond, and note any overlapping partner that is slow to return a bid.
Use the timing as the latency input when comparing an overlapping partner's two paths.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per exchange that appears on both paths, its win rate and revenue on the Open Bidding path, its win rate and revenue on the header bidding path, and the client-side latency it adds. An overlapping partner whose two paths were compared and whose configuration reflects that comparison passes. An overlapping partner running on both paths with no comparison of win rate, latency, or revenue is a fail recorded with the partner name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each overlapping partner's win rate, revenue, and latency on each path above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more exchanges run on {{site_name}} through both Open Bidding and header bidding without a comparison of the two paths. Routing the same partner server-side and client-side duplicates the auction work for that partner and can split its demand, and the two paths differ in win rate, latency, and fees, so keeping both without comparing them is a default rather than a decision. {{voice_recommend}} comparing each overlapping partner's win rate, client-side latency, and net revenue on each path, then settling on one deliberate path per partner or keeping both on purpose where the demand justifies it. After the change, confirm that each overlapping partner's path reflects the comparison rather than inertia.
